items.title: Complete
items.contentHtml:
Stanford A vs B

Stanford Classification

  • Stanford A: affects ascending aorta (with or without descending/arche). Surgical urgency (mortality ~1-2%/hour without surgery).
  • Stanford B: affects only descending aorta (distal to left subclavian). Initial medical management (beta blocker + pressure control).
  • Pearl examination: the DeBakey classification (I, II, III) is less used in emergencies; Stanford is the one that decides management.


HTN as a risk factor

High blood pressure

  • Main risk factor for aortic dissection (present in >70% of cases).
  • Chronic HTN generates degeneration of the medial layer (medial cystic necrosis) and weakens the wall aortic.
  • Examination trap: in young patients without HTN, think about connective tissue diseases (Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos) or bicuspid aortic valve.


Tearing pain

Chest pain

  • Classic characteristic: sudden, intense, tearing or lancinating, migratory (follows dissection path).
  • Location: anterior thoracic (type A) or interscapular (type B).
  • Critical DDx: AMI (pressing, non-migratory pain, with ECG changes); pulmonary embolism (pleuritic pain + dyspnea).


Chest X-ray: wide mediastinum

Chest ~60-70% (normal does not rule out).
  • Other signs: tracheal deviation to the right, left pleural effusion (blood).
  • Trap: Normal x-ray does not exclude dissection; If suspicion is high, proceed to CT angiography.


    • CT angiography: double lumen

    CT angiography (gold standard)

    • [Key finding]: double lumen sign (true and false lumen separated by intimal flap).
    • True lumen: smaller size, early homogeneous opacification. False lumen: larger, late and heterogeneous opacification.
    • Partial thrombus in false lumen is common (helps distinguish from penetrating ulcer).
    • Pearl: angio-CT with contrast has sensitivity and specificity >95% for diagnosis.


    Diagnostic TEE

    Transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE)

    • Indication: unstable patient or contraindication for CT (CRI, allergy).
    • Displays: intimal flap, differentiation of lumens, aortic insufficiency, pericardial effusion.
    • Sensitivity 98% for type A (less for distal type B).
    • Trap: requires expert operator; it does not clearly visualize the distal aortic arch or the proximal descending aorta (blind area due to the left bronchus).


    Type A surgery

    Stanford A surgical treatment

    • Urgent (emergency): ascending aorta replacement surgery with prosthetic graft (with or without coronary reimplantation).
    • Goal: to prevent aortic rupture, tamponade, acute aortic insufficiency.
    • Presurgical management: IV beta blocker (esmolol/labetalol) to reduce dP/dt, maintain SBP 100-120 mmHg.
    • Pearl: if there is severe aortic insufficiency, the valve is also replaced (Bentall).


    Type B medical

    Stanford B medical management

    • First line: IV beta blocker (esmolol, metoprolol or labetalol) to decrease dP/dt and heart rate (HR 60 bpm).
    • Goal: SBP 100-120 mmHg, HR 50-60 bpm.
    • If beta blocker contraindication: verapamil or diltiazem IV.
    • Indications for surgery/endoprosthesis in type B: complications (rupture, malperfusion, refractory hypertension, persistent pain).


    Cardiac tamponade

    Cardiac tamponade

    • Complication fatal type A: bleeding into the pericardium → diastolic compression of the right cavities.
    • Signs: hypotension, jugular engorgement, paradoxical pulse, auscultatory silence.
    • Echocardiography: pericardial effusion + diastolic collapse of the right atrium/ventricle.
    • Management: Rescue pericardiocentesis (only if extreme instability, can decompress and resume bleeding) + urgent surgery. cancer).
    • Utility: high negative predictive value (if normal D-dimer and low clinical suspicion, can rule out dissection).
    • Trap examination: do not use to confirm; If suspicion is high, go directly to imaging (CT angiography/TEE).

items.title: Stanford A vs B
items.contentHtml:
Stanford Classification

  • Stanford A: affects ascending aorta (with or without descending/arche). Surgical urgency (mortality ~1-2%/hour without surgery).
  • Stanford B: affects only descending aorta (distal to left subclavian). Initial medical management (beta blocker + pressure control).
  • Pearl examination: the DeBakey classification (I, II, III) is less used in emergencies; Stanford is the one that decides management.

items.title: HTN as a risk factor
items.contentHtml:
High blood pressure

  • Main risk factor for aortic dissection (present in >70% of cases).
  • Chronic HTN generates degeneration of the medial layer (medial cystic necrosis) and weakens the aortic wall.
  • Examination trap: in young patients without HTN, think about connective tissue diseases (Sd. Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos) or bicuspid aortic valve.

items.title: tearing pain
items.contentHtml:
Chest pain

  • Classic characteristic: pain sudden, intense, tearing or lancinating, migratory (follows dissection path).
  • Location: anterior thoracic (type A) or interscapular (type B).
  • Critical DDx: AMI (pressing, non-migratory pain, with ECG changes); pulmonary embolism (pleuritic pain + dyspnea).

items.title: Chest x-ray: wide mediastinum
items.contentHtml:
Chest (blood).
  • Trap: Normal x-ray does not exclude dissection; If suspicion is high, proceed to CT angiography.

items.foco: Double lumen sign on CT angiography
items.title: CT angiography: double light
items.contentHtml:
Angio-CT (gold standard)

  • [Key finding]: double lumen sign (true and false lumen separated by intimal flap).
  • True lumen: smaller size, early homogeneous opacification. False lumen: larger, late and heterogeneous opacification.
  • Partial thrombus in false lumen is common (helps distinguish from penetrating ulcer).
  • Pearl: CT angiography with contrast has sensitivity and specificity >95% for diagnosis.

items.foco: Transesophageal echocardiogram as a diagnostic tool
items.title: diagnostic TEE
items.contentHtml:
Transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE)

  • Indication: unstable patient or contraindication for CT (CRI, allergy).
  • Displays: intimal flap, differentiation of lumens, aortic insufficiency, pericardial effusion.
  • Sensitivity 98% for type A (lower for type B distal).
  • Trap: requires expert operator; it does not clearly visualize the distal aortic arch or the proximal descending aorta (blind area for the left bronchus).

items.foco: Urgent surgical treatment in type A
items.title: Type A surgery
items.contentHtml:
Stanford A surgical treatment

  • Urgent (emergency): ascending aorta replacement surgery with prosthetic graft (with or without coronary reimplantation).
  • Goal: prevent aortic rupture, tamponade, acute aortic insufficiency.
  • Presurgical management: IV beta blocker (esmolol/labetalol) to reduce dP/dt, maintain SBP 100-120 mmHg.
  • Pearl: if there is severe aortic insufficiency, the valve is also replaced (Bentall).

items.foco: Medical management with beta blockers in type B
items.title: Type B doctor
items.contentHtml:
Medical management Stanford B

  • First line: IV beta blocker (esmolol, metoprolol or labetalol) to reduce dP/dt and heart rate (HR 60 bpm).
  • Goal: SBP 100-120 mmHg, HR 50-60 bpm.
  • If beta blocker contraindication: verapamil or diltiazem iv.
  • Indications for surgery/endoprosthesis in type B: complications (rupture, malperfusion, refractory hypertension, persistent pain).

items.title: cardiac tamponade
items.contentHtml:
Cardiac tamponade

  • Fatal complication type A: bleeding into the pericardium → diastolic compression of the right cavities.
  • Signs: hypotension, jugular engorgement, paradoxical pulse, auscultatory silence.
  • Echocardiography: pericardial effusion + diastolic collapse of right atrium/ventricle.
  • Management: rescue pericardiocentesis (only if extreme instability, can decompress and resume bleeding) + urgent surgery.

items.title: D-dimer
items.contentHtml:
D-dimer

  • Elevated (generally >500 ng/mL) in acute aortic dissection, but nonspecific (also in PE, AMI, pregnancy, cancer).
  • Usefulness: high negative predictive value (if normal D-dimer and low clinical suspicion, it can rule out dissection).
  • Trap examination: no use to confirm; If suspicion is high, go directly to imaging (CT angiography/TEE).
